novel_features.name: Closest open shelter, including pet-friendly
novel_features.description: Ranks open shelters by straight-line distance from a lat,lon, ZIP, or street address; geocodes shelters that are missing coordinates and reports any it cannot locate rather than dropping them.
novel_features.rationale: The feed frequently omits coordinates even for open shelters, so a useful 'closest shelter' answer requires client-side geocoding (free US Census), distance ranking, and honest handling of unlocatable rows.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Answers 'the closest shelter to me that allows pets' in one call; add --pets, --ada, or --wheelchair.

novel_features.name: Honest capacity, never a fabricated denominator
novel_features.description: Computes utilization only where both a population and a capacity are reported, labeling the denominator (evacuation vs post-impact), and surfaces shelters reported FULL without asserting it as ground truth.
novel_features.rationale: Capacity numbers are mostly null; a safety tool must never invent a denominator or assume a shelter is full or empty.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Answers 'which shelter is at capacity?' honestly, marking unknowns as unknown.

novel_features.name: One-call situational briefing
novel_features.description: One command returns the open count, a breakdown by state, pet-friendly and accessible counts, and the capacity picture, with an optional human briefing.
novel_features.rationale: A single compound command gives an agent the whole shelter situation without four separate calls.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this first when an agent asks 'what is the shelter situation right now'.

novel_features.name: Filtered open-shelter listing
novel_features.description: Open shelters flattened from the feed and filterable by state, pets, ADA, wheelchair, managing org, and status.
novel_features.rationale: The raw ArcGIS feature feed nests fields under attributes and is not directly filterable; this flattens and filters it for agents and people.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use to narrow open shelters to the ones that match a person's needs.

novel_features.name: Flattened shelter detail by stable id
novel_features.description: Full detail for one shelter joined on the stable shelter_id rather than the churning objectid, with unreported fields as explicit null.
novel_features.rationale: objectid is unstable across snapshots; joining on shelter_id is the only correct way to reference one shelter.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use when you have a shelter_id and need its full record.

narrative.headline: Credible, real-time disaster shelter information for AI agents and people: which FEMA-tracked shelters are open right now, where they are, which take pets, and which are at capacity. Sourced from FEMA's National Shelter System OpenShelters feed, honest about missing data.
narrative.value_prop: Gives agents and people the most credible open-shelter picture straight from FEMA's National Shelter System (NSS) OpenShelters feed: open shelters across all states and territories, who takes pets, who is wheelchair / ADA accessible, and who is filling up. It answers the questions people actually ask in a disaster, like 'the closest open shelter to me that allows pets' and 'which shelters are at capacity', geocoding addresses when the feed omits coordinates and never inventing a number it does not have. Deep thanks to all first responders, emergency management practitioners, and relief nonprofit organizations for the work you do in communities when disaster strikes. This is an unofficial tool; in a life-threatening emergency call 911 and follow the official guidance and evacuation orders from FEMA, your local emergency management, and your local authorities.

quickstart.comment: Confirm the CLI is wired up; no API key needed.
What shelters are open right now (few or none when no disaster is active).

quickstart.command: shelters-pp-cli near "2400 W Bradley Ave, Champaign, IL" --pets
quickstart.comment: The closest open shelters to me that allow pets.
One-call situational briefing with the gratitude and safety footer.

troubleshoots.fix: That is the normal quiet-state contract (no disaster active). It is not an error.

troubleshoots.symptom: near says a shelter could not be located
troubleshoots.fix: The feed omitted coordinates and the address would not geocode; those shelters are reported in a count and excluded from the ranking. Pass a precise lat,lon origin for the most accurate distances.

troubleshoots.symptom: capacity shows unknown for a shelter
troubleshoots.fix: The feed did not report both a population and a capacity for it; this tool will not invent a denominator.

narrative.when_to_use: Use shelters-pp-cli when an AI agent or operator needs trustworthy, current information about open disaster shelters: where they are, which take pets, which are accessible, and which are at or near capacity. It is ideal for situational-awareness assistants, disaster-response tooling, and anyone helping a person find a safe place to shelter during an emergency.
anti_triggers: Do not use this CLI as a substitute for 911 or official evacuation orders; defer to FEMA, local emergency management, and local authorities.
Do not treat a shelter's status as real-time; the feed updates roughly twice a day and may lag reality.
Do not expect full National Shelter System data; only the public OpenShelters feed is covered (full NSS requires an MOU with FEMA).

recipes.title: Closest pet-friendly shelter as compact JSON for an agent
recipes.explanation: Returns just the nearest pet-friendly shelter so an agent does not burn context on the full feed.

recipes.title: Shelters confirmed wheelchair accessible in one state
recipes.explanation: Filters open shelters to those confirmed wheelchair accessible.

recipes.title: Which shelters are at or over capacity
recipes.explanation: Reports utilization where computable and flags reported-full shelters, honestly marking unknowns.

recipes.title: One-call situational briefing
recipes.explanation: Bundles open count, by-state, accessibility, and capacity into a single human-readable briefing.
